questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: What discipline does a stack enforce on insert and remove? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Push adds to the top; pop removes from the top—classic LIFO.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which pair of operations defines the core stack ADT? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Stacks expose push and pop at one end; peek reads the top without removal.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Popping an empty stack should typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Signal an error/underflow (exception or error code)
questions.explanation: Underflow is an exceptional condition; well-defined APIs fail loudly or return Optional.empty.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A call stack in a running program primarily stores:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Activation records: return addresses, locals, parameters, saved registers
questions.explanation: Each call pushes a frame; return pops it—hence stack overflow on deep recursion.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Implementing a stack with a dynamic array (vector) makes push amortized:? Which answer is best?
questions.explanation: Append at end is amortized O(1); rare geometric resizes cost O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Implementing a stack with a singly linked list (push/pop at head) gives:? Which answer is best?
options: O(1) push and pop without shifting elements
O(log n) due to tree balancing
questions.explanation: Head insert/delete is constant time; no contiguous shifting.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Balanced parentheses checking uses a stack because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: FIFO matches nested openers to closers
The most recent unmatched opener must match the next closer (LIFO nesting)
questions.explanation: Nesting is LIFO: the latest open bracket is what the next close must match.

questions.stem: Which sequence of stack operations leaves the stack empty? Start empty. push(1), push(2), pop(), push(3), pop(), pop().
questions.explanation: After ops: [1,2] → pop→[1] → push3→[1,3] → pop→[1] → pop→[].

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Peek/top on a non-empty stack returns:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The most recently pushed element still present
questions.explanation: Top is the LIFO end—the last pushed item that has not been popped.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Stack overflow in recursion usually means:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The heap ran out of disk swap only
Call depth exceeded available call-stack memory
questions.explanation: Each frame consumes stack space; unbounded or huge depth exhausts the call stack.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Infix to postfix conversion algorithms commonly rely on a stack to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Hold operators until precedence/associativity says to emit them
questions.explanation: Shunting-yard uses an operator stack guided by precedence and parentheses.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Evaluating a postfix expression efficiently uses a stack of:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Operands; when an operator appears, pop operands, push result
questions.explanation: Postfix is stack-friendly: operands push; operators consume and push back.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Compared to a queue, a stack is a better fit for:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Undo history and nested scope matching
questions.explanation: Undo and nesting are inherently LIFO; BFS and fair queues need FIFO.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A fixed-capacity array stack that pushes when full should:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Grow the array without telling anyone it was fixed
questions.explanation: Bounded stacks define overflow behavior; silent corruption is unacceptable.

questions.stem: Which statement about Java’s Stack class vs Deque-as-stack is a common interview note?
options: Stack is always preferred as a legacy Vector subclass; Deque is never used
Prefer ArrayDeque (or Deque) for stack use; java.util.Stack is a legacy synchronized Vector
Stack is lock-free and Deque is not
questions.explanation: Modern Java guidance: use Deque methods push/pop/peek; Stack is outdated.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Space complexity of storing n elements in a stack is:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: You must retain all n items until popped—Θ(n) memory.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Reversing a string or array segment with a stack works because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Push all, then pop all yields reverse order
questions.explanation: LIFO naturally reverses a sequence.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: In browser history “back,” a stack of pages models:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The next pages you have not visited yet (forward often uses another stack)
questions.explanation: Back pops the previous page; forward is often a second stack.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which is NOT a typical stack application? Which response is most accurate?
options: DFS traversal with an explicit stack
Shortest path in an unweighted graph via BFS
questions.explanation: Unweighted shortest path uses a queue (BFS), not a stack.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Ambiguous interview phrase “stack overflow” might mean:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Either call-stack exhaustion or a bounded stack ADT overflow—clarify context
questions.explanation: Same words, two meanings: OS/runtime call stack vs ADT capacity—ask which.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: A monotonic increasing stack (storing indices) is typically used to find:? Which answer is best?
options: Next smaller element to the right (by popping larger candidates)
Only the global maximum in O(n²)
questions.explanation: While the new element breaks monotonicity, pop; popped indices find their next smaller/greater depending on direction.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Next Greater Element for each array index in O(n) uses:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A stack of indices awaiting a greater value; when found, resolve popped indices
Sorting the array in place permanently
A queue of all greater candidates
questions.explanation: Monotonic stack processes each index at most twice (push/pop)—linear time.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Min-stack (getMin in O(1) with push/pop O(1)) commonly stores:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Only the current min in a global variable without history
A full sorted copy of all elements
Each push paired with the min-so-far, or a parallel mins stack
A heap alone without a stack
questions.explanation: Track minima history so pop can restore the previous min in O(1).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Daily Temperatures / “days until warmer” is solved like NGE by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Binary search on sorted temperatures only
Monotonic stack of indices of unresolved colder days
questions.explanation: Same next-greater pattern: when a warmer day appears, answer pops.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Largest rectangle in histogram (classic) uses a stack to:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Find, for each bar, previous/next smaller bars to compute max width × height
Store bar heights in sorted order only
Convert histogram to a balanced BST
questions.explanation: Near-smallest boundaries via monotonic stack give O(n) area computation.

questions.stem: Validating a path of push/pop sequences (permutation is a valid stack permutation) checks:
options: Whether pops could arise from some push order using a stack simulation
Whether a queue could produce it instead
questions.explanation: Simulate: push when needed, pop when top matches next desired output; detect impossibility.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Implementing a queue using two stacks typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Amortizes transfers: enqueue to in-stack, dequeue from out-stack (refill by pouring in→out)
questions.explanation: Lazy transfer yields amortized O(1) per op while preserving FIFO.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Asteroid collision problems use a stack because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: All asteroids collide simultaneously with no order
A min-heap picks the smallest asteroid
Only the most recent surviving asteroid can collide with the incoming one
questions.explanation: Collisions resolve against the top of the “surviving” line—stack simulation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Decode string patterns like 3[a2[c]] often use stacks to store:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Counts and partial strings to expand nested repeats
questions.explanation: Nesting of k[…] maps to push on ‘[’ and pop/expand on ‘]’.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Simplifying a Unix path (/, .., .) is stack-friendly because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Paths are FIFO queues of mounts
“..” pops the previous directory segment when possible
questions.explanation: Directory nesting + parent (..) is exactly stack push/pop of path parts.

questions.stem: Trapping rain water (two-pointer or stack variants): the stack approach tracks:
options: Indices of decreasing heights to form basins when a higher bar appears
DFS on a tree of bars
questions.explanation: Monotonic decreasing heights; a rise closes valleys and adds trapped water.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When using a stack for iterative DFS, what do you push? Which response is most accurate?
options: Nodes to visit (often with state), popping to process, pushing neighbors
questions.explanation: Explicit stack replaces the call stack; careful with visit timing vs recursion.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A max-stack (O(1) getMax) mirrors min-stack by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Storing max-so-far alongside each push or on an auxiliary stack
Scanning the whole stack each getMax
questions.explanation: Same history trick as min-stack, tracking maxima.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Basic calculator with +, -, and parentheses often combines:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A stack of signs/results when entering parentheses, then apply on exit
A single integer with no stack
questions.explanation: Parentheses create nested contexts; stacks restore prior sign/sum.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Remove k digits to form the smallest number uses a monotonic stack to:? Which answer is best?
options: Always delete the last k digits
Sort digits ascending and drop k largest only without order
Keep digits ascending when possible by removing peaks that hurt smallness
Use a max-heap of indices randomly
questions.explanation: Greedy: pop larger digits before a smaller one while removals remain.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why store indices instead of values in many monotonic-stack problems? Which response is most accurate?
options: You need positions to compute distances/widths, and values are available via array[index]
questions.explanation: Answers often need index differences; the array still holds values.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Exclusive time of functions in a call log uses a stack of:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Function ids with timestamps to attribute exclusive CPU between events
questions.explanation: Nested calls are LIFO; stack tracks who is running between start/end events.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Stock span problem (consecutive days ≤ today’s price) is solved with:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Monotonic stack of prior days’ prices/indices
Counting sort of all prices globally
questions.explanation: Find previous greater; span derives from index distance—monotonic stack.

questions.stem: Designing an O(1) amortized stack that also supports getMin, a subtle bug is:
options: Forgetting to pop the mins stack when the popped value equals current min
questions.explanation: Mins auxiliary structure must stay synced on duplicate minima and pops.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Parsing nested JSON-like braces with a stack fails validation when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A closer appears while stack empty, or final stack non-empty, or type mismatch on pop
questions.explanation: Same rules as brackets: underflow, leftover opens, or wrong closer type.

questions.stem: Senior pitfall: recursive DFS “works” until production graphs are deep. What do you ship?
options: Iterative DFS/BFS with heap-allocated explicit stack/queue and cycle guards
Increase stack size only and ignore
questions.explanation: Call-stack limits are real; explicit structures scale and fail more controllably.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Monotonic stack off-by-one on circular arrays (NGE II) usually needs:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Simulating 2n traversal (mod n) while still pushing each index once logically
A third stack for the wrap
questions.explanation: Visit indices twice around the ring; avoid double-pushing resolved indices.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Min-stack storing (value − min) encoded in one stack risks:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Integer overflow/underflow and harder debugging versus a clear aux stack
questions.explanation: Clever encodings save space but invite overflow and maintenance bugs—know the trade-off.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Using Stack in a multi-threaded server without synchronization causes:? Which answer is best?
options: Data races / corruption unless the structure is concurrent or externally locked
questions.explanation: Classic stacks are not thread-safe; ConcurrentLinkedDeque or locks are needed.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why can “two stacks make a queue” still be a poor production queue? Which response is most accurate?
options: Worse constants, poorer locality, and awkward concurrency versus a ring buffer or ArrayDeque
questions.explanation: Pedagogically fine; real systems prefer contiguous circular buffers.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Expression evaluator security pitfall with unbounded nesting:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Deep parentheses can exhaust memory/CPU—enforce depth limits and input size
questions.explanation: Untrusted input + unbounded stack growth is a DoS vector.

questions.stem: Iterator invalidation: holding a pointer into a vector-backed stack across push that resizes leads to:
options: Dangling references / UB in C++; stale views in languages that reallocate backing stores
questions.explanation: Resizing invalidates raw pointers/iterators into the old buffer.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Claiming every LIFO problem needs a monotonic stack is wrong because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Many LIFO tasks are plain simulation; monotonicity applies to next-greater/smaller style constraints
questions.explanation: Pick the pattern that matches the invariant—don’t force monotonicity everywhere.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: In lock-free concurrent stacks, the hard part is often:? Choose the strongest option.
options: ABA problems and safe memory reclamation after pop
questions.explanation: CAS on head is the easy sketch; ABA and reclamation dominate real designs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Production undo stack that also needs redo typically uses:? Choose the strongest option.
options: One stack only, discarding redo forever
Undo stack + redo stack (or a single list with a cursor); clears redo on new action
questions.explanation: Dual-stack or cursor models are standard; new edits invalidate redo branch.
